verses.text: Word reaches Joseph: your father is sick. He takes his two sons, Manasseh and Ephraim, and goes to see him. When Jacob hears Joseph is coming, he gathers his strength and sits up on the bed. Even dying, the patriarch rises for the son he loves.

Word reaches Joseph: your father is sick. He takes his two sons, Manasseh and Ephraim, and goes to see him. When Jacob hears Joseph is coming, he gathers his strength and sits up on the bed. Even dying, the patriarch rises for the son he loves.

Jacob tells Joseph: God Almighty appeared to me at Luz in Canaan and blessed me. He said: I will make you fruitful and multiply you and give this land to your offspring as an everlasting possession. The old vision at Bethel is what shapes this farewell speech.

Jacob tells Joseph: God Almighty appeared to me at Luz in Canaan and blessed me. He said: I will make you fruitful and multiply you and give this land to your offspring as an everlasting possession. The old vision at Bethel is what shapes this farewell speech.

Now your two sons born to you in Egypt before I came — Ephraim and Manasseh — are mine. They will be reckoned to me like Reuben and Simeon. Any children you have after them are yours. Jacob is adopting his grandsons, raising them to tribe-level inheritance.

Now your two sons born to you in Egypt before I came — Ephraim and Manasseh — are mine. They will be reckoned to me like Reuben and Simeon. Any children you have after them are yours. Jacob is adopting his grandsons, raising them to tribe-level inheritance.

When I came from Padan, Rachel died beside me in Canaan, on the way, with still a little distance to Ephrath. I buried her there on the road. The dying patriarch is still grieving the wife he lost. Some wounds are never closed.
Israel sees Joseph's sons: who are these? Joseph: they are my sons, whom God has given me here. Bring them to me, I will bless them. He has been waiting for this moment.
Israel sees Joseph's sons: who are these? Joseph: they are my sons, whom God has given me here. Bring them to me, I will bless them. He has been waiting for this moment.
Israel's eyes are dim with age; he can hardly see. Joseph brings the boys close. Jacob kisses them and embraces them. The old man holds the next generation against his chest.
I had not thought to see your face, says Jacob — and look, God has shown me your offspring also. The grief he gave up on twenty-some years ago has been answered with more than he asked for.

Joseph removes the boys from between his father's knees and bows his face to the ground. He brings them forward: Ephraim toward Israel's left, Manasseh toward Israel's right — the firstborn naturally placed under the dominant hand.

Joseph removes the boys from between his father's knees and bows his face to the ground. He brings them forward: Ephraim toward Israel's left, Manasseh toward Israel's right — the firstborn naturally placed under the dominant hand.

But Israel stretches out his right hand and lays it on Ephraim's head — the younger — and his left on Manasseh, crossing his arms intentionally. He knows what he is doing. Manasseh is the firstborn, but the pattern of Genesis runs the other way.

Then Jacob blesses Joseph: the God before whom my fathers Abraham and Isaac walked, the God who has shepherded me all my life, the Angel who redeemed me from all evil — bless the lads. Let my name be named on them. Let them grow into a multitude in the midst of the earth. Jacob credits God with shepherding him through everything — including the parts he made hard himself.

Then Jacob blesses Joseph: the God before whom my fathers Abraham and Isaac walked, the God who has shepherded me all my life, the Angel who redeemed me from all evil — bless the lads. Let my name be named on them. Let them grow into a multitude in the midst of the earth. Jacob credits God with shepherding him through everything — including the parts he made hard himself.

When Joseph sees the right hand on Ephraim, it displeases him. He takes his father's hand to move it: not so, my father — this one is the firstborn. Put your right hand here. Joseph still expects the old rule to apply.

When Joseph sees the right hand on Ephraim, it displeases him. He takes his father's hand to move it: not so, my father — this one is the firstborn. Put your right hand here. Joseph still expects the old rule to apply.

Jacob refuses: I know, my son, I know. He shall also become a people, and shall be great. But his younger brother shall be greater than he, and his offspring shall become a multitude of nations. Jacob has lived through this pattern himself.

That day Jacob blesses them, saying: by you Israel will bless their sons, saying, God make you as Ephraim and Manasseh. He sets Ephraim before Manasseh. The younger goes first, even in the words other parents will speak.

Israel says to Joseph: I am dying. God shall be with you and bring you back to the land of your fathers. And I have given you one portion above your brothers — Shechem — which I took from the Amorite with sword and bow. The patriarch dies with the promise of return on his lips.

Israel says to Joseph: I am dying. God shall be with you and bring you back to the land of your fathers. And I have given you one portion above your brothers — Shechem — which I took from the Amorite with sword and bow. The patriarch dies with the promise of return on his lips.
